domain_summary: Dark matter is an invisible, hypothetical form of matter that comprises approximately 80% of all matter and 30.1% of the universe's total matter-energy content, detected only through gravitational effects. The three leading theoretical candidates are WIMPs (1 GeV/c² - 10⁵ GeV/c² mass range, weak nuclear interactions), axions (microeV mass, solving the strong CP problem via Peccei-Quinn mechanism), and sterile neutrinos (keV mass range, gravitational-only interactions). As of 2025-2026, direct detection experiments have dramatically improved sensitivity with LUX-ZEPLIN achieving 2.2×10⁻⁴⁸ cm² WIMP cross-section limits and ADMX excluding specific axion mass ranges, while axions are increasingly favored as the leading candidate due to lack of WIMP detection success. The field has reached the neutrino floor for WIMP detection, driving investment toward alternative candidate searches.

claims.claim: Dark matter is an invisible, hypothetical form of matter that does not interact with light or other electromagnetic radiation
claims.notes: Consistent definition across multiple authoritative sources

claims.claim: Dark matter makes up approximately 80% of all matter in the universe
Dark matter comprises 30.1% of the total matter-energy composition of the universe (with dark energy at 69.4% and ordinary matter at 0.5%)
claims.notes: Specific percentage breakdown from peer-reviewed reference

claims.claim: Dark matter's presence is detected through its gravitational effects rather than direct observation
claims.notes: Foundational principle of dark matter identification

claims.claim: Dark matter and ordinary matter co-existed and evolved together throughout cosmic history
claims.notes: Based on recent James Webb Space Telescope observations from January 2026

claims.claim: WIMPs (Weakly Interacting Massive Particles) have predicted mass ranges from 1 GeV/c² to 10⁵ GeV/c² with typical focus on 100 GeV/c² to TeV scale
claims.notes: Mass scale emerges naturally from electroweak symmetry breaking

claims.claim: WIMPs interact via the weak nuclear force with interaction cross sections ranging from 10⁻⁴¹ to 10⁻⁵¹ cm², and require self-annihilation cross sections of approximately 3×10⁻²⁶ cm³/s to achieve correct dark matter abundance
claims.notes: Well-established theoretical prediction linked to thermal relic abundance

claims.claim: Axions are predicted to have very low masses in the microeV range (much smaller than other candidates) and couple to photons and gluons through the Peccei-Quinn mechanism, which solves the strong CP problem in QCD
Sterile neutrinos are predicted to have keV/c² mass range (1-100 keV) and interact extremely weakly through gravity only, making them distinct from active neutrinos
claims.notes: KATRIN TRISTAN upgrade operational 2026 targets this mass range

claims.claim: As of 2025-2026, axions are increasingly cited as the leading dark matter candidate, with some physicists calling it 'the most likely dark matter candidate today,' due to lack of confirmed WIMP detection signals
claims.notes: Direct detection experiments reached neutrino floor without WIMP confirmation

claims.claim: Direct detection of WIMPs has entered the 'neutrino-floor era' as of 2025, where solar neutrino backgrounds limit further sensitivity improvements with current technology
claims.notes: Reported at ICRC 2025 conference by XENONnT and PandaX-4T

claims.claim: XENONnT achieves spin-independent WIMP-nucleon cross section limit of 1.7×10⁻⁴⁷ cm² at 30 GeV/c² WIMP mass (90% CL) with 3.1 tonne-year exposure
LUX-ZEPLIN (LZ) sets most stringent spin-independent WIMP limits of 2.2×10⁻⁴⁸ cm² at 43 GeV/c² WIMP mass (90% CL) as of December 2025
ADMX excludes QCD axion parameter space in frequency range 1.10-1.31 GHz (4.54-5.41 μeV axion mass) for plausible QCD axion models (2025 results)
ADMX also excludes Dine-Fischler-Srednicki-Zhitnitsky (DFSZ) axions between 3.27-3.31 μeV at 90% confidence level (2025 results)
claims.notes: DFSZ model represents extended axion framework with different coupling constants

knowledge_gaps.gap: The exact composition and identity of dark matter particles remains unknown with no direct detection confirmed
Specific 2026 SuperCDMS sensitivity numbers and low-mass WIMP cross-section limits not quantified
Current 2026 experimental constraints on sterile neutrino parameter space largely unexplored despite KATRIN TRISTAN upgrade
Specific coupling constants and detailed interaction mechanisms for axion-photon and axion-gluon couplings not fully quantified
Impact of neutrino fog on future WIMP discovery potential and fundamental physics limitations not addressed
Combined parameter space visualization showing how different experiments complement/overlap not provided
